finder_output.summary: Both documents require board and senior management oversight of technology and cyber risks, with our open finance governance deferring to RMiT-style risk management frameworks.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Our open finance policy explicitly requires FSPs to observe RMiT technology risk requirements for their open finance activities, adopting the RMiT framework without narrowing.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Both require robust cyber risk management, API security controls, fraud detection and monitoring for digital/open finance services.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Both require multi-factor authentication and secure authentication procedures before granting access to customer information/critical systems.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Both require third-party service provider due diligence, SLA safeguards and confidentiality obligations for handling customer information.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Both require business continuity, operational resilience and continuity of services against disruptions, outages and third-party failures.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Both require breach handling and incident response plans with escalation procedures for customer information/cyber incidents.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Both require internal audit coverage extended to open finance/technology activities and API integrations.
finder_output.label: aligns-with

finder_output.summary: Our open finance policy sets a detailed consent lifecycle regime (specific/voluntary consent, dashboards, time-bound validity, revocation, deletion) that RMiT does not address.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

finder_output.summary: Our open finance policy defines mandated participation as data provider/consumer with reciprocity, prescribed information scope and required interfaces which RMiT does not cover.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

finder_output.summary: Our open finance policy addresses customer complaints, liability delineation, and fair treatment/redress specific to open finance arrangements which RMiT does not cover.
finder_output.label: goes-beyond

finder_output.summary: RMiT prescribes detailed technical controls (cryptography, data centre resilience, network resilience, SDLC, patch/EOL, access control) that our open finance policy relies on but does not itself detail.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: RMiT sets specific service availability standards (downtime limits, stand-in processing, monitoring thresholds) for digital services that our open finance policy does not quantify.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: RMiT requires notification/consultation with the Bank for new digital services and cloud/emerging technology adoption which our open finance policy does not separately address.
finder_output.label: silent-on

finder_output.summary: RMiT prescribes detailed digital fraud management and customer awareness/kill-switch requirements which our open finance policy addresses only at a high level through cross-reference.
finder_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: Both documents require board and senior management oversight of technology and cyber risks, with our open finance governance deferring to RMiT-style risk management frameworks.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: Open finance governance obligations apply per-FSP as may be applicable in relevant policy documents (para 7.2), while RMiT scales expectations to size, complexity and degree of digitalisation (RMiT 1.2).

critic_output.summary: Our open finance policy explicitly requires FSPs to observe RMiT technology risk requirements for their open finance activities, adopting the RMiT framework without narrowing.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: Open finance cross-references RMiT and Technology Requirement for Payment Services Regulatees; RMiT itself excludes certain entities (eligible e-money issuers, non-bank merchant acquirers, intermediary remittance institutions) from some provisions (RMiT 2.2).

critic_output.summary: Both require robust cyber risk management, API security controls, fraud detection and monitoring for digital/open finance services.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: Both require multi-factor authentication and secure authentication procedures before granting access to customer information/critical systems.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: Open finance frames MFA as an example ('e.g. multi-factor authentication') for a data provider authenticating the customer, whereas RMiT 10.55 mandates MFA for critical system access as a firm requirement.

critic_output.summary: Both require third-party service provider due diligence, SLA safeguards and confidentiality obligations for handling customer information.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: Open finance defers to the Outsourcing, RMiT and Management of Customer Information policy documents; RMiT's detailed SLA content (RMiT 10.48) applies specifically to third parties for critical technology functions.

critic_output.summary: Both require business continuity, operational resilience and continuity of services against disruptions, outages and third-party failures.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: Both require breach handling and incident response plans with escalation procedures for customer information/cyber incidents.
critic_output.label: aligns-with
critic_output.scope_note: Open finance breach handling cross-refers to the Management of Customer Information and Permitted Disclosures policy and PDPA; RMiT 11.18 governs cyber incident notification to the Bank.

critic_output.summary: Both require internal audit coverage extended to open finance/technology activities and API integrations.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: Our open finance policy sets a detailed consent lifecycle regime (specific/voluntary consent, dashboards, time-bound validity, revocation, deletion) that RMiT does not address.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: Our open finance policy defines mandated participation as data provider/consumer with reciprocity, prescribed information scope and required interfaces which RMiT does not cover.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: Our open finance policy addresses customer complaints, liability delineation, and fair treatment/redress specific to open finance arrangements which RMiT does not cover.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond

critic_output.summary: RMiT prescribes detailed technical controls (cryptography, data centre resilience, network resilience, SDLC, patch/EOL, access control) that our open finance policy relies on but does not itself detail.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: RMiT sets specific service availability standards (downtime limits, stand-in processing, monitoring thresholds) for digital services that our open finance policy does not quantify.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: RMiT requires notification/consultation with the Bank for new digital services and cloud/emerging technology adoption which our open finance policy does not separately address.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: RMiT prescribes detailed digital fraud management and customer awareness/kill-switch requirements which our open finance policy addresses only at a high level through cross-reference.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: Both require that customer information received from or handled via another party be protected by safeguards at least equivalent to those applied to the institution's own information.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: Both mandate least-privilege / need-to-know access restrictions to customer information and IT assets.
critic_output.label: aligns-with

critic_output.summary: Both require data access / activity logging retained for a defined minimum period, but our open finance policy sets a seven-year retention for consent and access records while RMiT sets three years for user activity and network logs.
critic_output.label: differs-on

critic_output.summary: RMiT prescribes detailed system backup, restoration and tamper-proof recovery requirements to meet business recovery objectives which our open finance policy does not separately detail.
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: RMiT requires a designated CISO and an independent enterprise-wide technology risk management function, which our open finance policy does not itself require (relying on FSP-level senior management responsibility instead).
critic_output.label: silent-on

critic_output.summary: Our open finance policy requires secure disposal/deletion of customer information upon expiry of use or revoked consent, aligning with RMiT's SDLC and data protection controls but adding a consent-triggered deletion obligation.
critic_output.label: goes-beyond
